entities.description: A composite English and Latin anthology assembled in six booklets in the second half of the fifteenth century. The target compilation occupies fols. 54v–55r as manuscript items 8 and 9; several scribes write fols. 54v–168r in Secretary script, and fols. 54v–57r use red brackets and red initial letters.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. poet. 32

physicalDescription.dimensions: Leaves approximately 280 × 195 mm; binding approximately 280 × 200 mm
physicalDescription.extent: ii modern paper flyleaves, ii parchment leaves foliated 1–2, and 206 folios; fols. 205–206 are flyleaves and fol. 206 is modern paper
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex anthology in six booklets
notes: The current non-medieval binding is white leather over pasteboard with four barely raised spine bands.
The catalog gives the manuscript's origin as unknown and identifies the scribal language of the relevant section broadly with western Leicestershire or Warwickshire.
A leaf is lost after fol. 53 and fol. 54r is blank; the target starts on fol. 54v.
Richard Turnowre wrote his name on fol. 205r around 1500.

relations.subjectLabel: Edden and Thompson, Middle English Verse Proverbs (2000)

reviewNotes: The exact cataloged medieval subject is the 168-line Rawlinson compilation. Zupitza's German introduction and notes are encoded editorial matter, not intellectual components of the medieval work.

Zupitza says the work is probably not unified: he treats lines 129–136 as a later addition, probably connects lines 137–146 with that addition, identifies lines 147–156 as a still younger Ten Commandments section, and describes lines 157–168 as appended teachings. Those are editorial arguments rather than separately dated physical strata established by the manuscript catalog.

DIMEV describes Prouerbis of Wysdom as 106 lines in couplets, while the Rawlinson witness's main rubricated component contains 146 lines before the Ten Commandments. The discrepancy reflects the fluid, manuscript-specific proverb tradition and remains explicit.

Edden and Thompson recommend treating each proverb compilation as a separate item and the organizing role as compilation rather than assuming a single stable authorial work. Their witness list says Rawlinson poet. 33, an evident shelfmark typo for poet. 32.
The Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries record cites Zupitza 1867 for the target item; Zupitza's scanned article and journal title page establish the relevant publication as 1893.

The complete Digital Bodleian published inventory was visually audited: image 1 is fol. 30r and image 2 is fol. 57r, at 1800 × 2734 and 1800 × 2751 pixels. Neither image contains the work on fols. 54v–55r.

Digital Bodleian metadata names Sulpicius as author and John Lydgate as translator for other contents of the composite manuscript. Those inherited labels are not responsibilities for this proverb compilation.

Whole-object completeness is evaluated against the physical manuscript, not the target work. A future supplied facsimile should be cached as a complete manuscript object and then given a separate work_portion for fols. 54v–55r.

lineage.summary: CME00074 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic transcription of Julius Zupitza's 1893 article The Prouerbis of Wysdom in Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 90, pp. 241–268. The article directly transcribes Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. poet. 32, fols. 54v–55r: a 146-line proverb compilation, a separately rubricated ten-line Ten Commandments text, and twelve appended moral lines. Zupitza's German introduction and extensive notes are also encoded, so the digital item is broader than the medieval compilation. A complete 500-page scan of the journal volume is cached and maps the article to PDF pages 255–282. The official Digital Bodleian object publishes only two selected manuscript images, fols. 30r and 57r; the cache contains that complete two-canvas published selection, but it has no overlap with the target fols. 54v–55r and remains partial manuscript coverage. No complete public digital facsimile of the manuscript was found. The whole-object request route therefore asks for the complete current physical manuscript—including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges where offered—and maps the target work separately to fols. 54v–55r.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Compilation and first known attestation: second half of the fifteenth century (1450–1500); exact composition dates unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)

catalogingSubject.label: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact 168-line Middle English compilation copied continuously in Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. poet. 32, fols. 54v–55r, printed by Julius Zupitza in 1893, and encoded as the medieval-text division of CME00074. It comprises a 146-line proverb compilation under the manuscript rubric This folowyng ben the Prouerbis of Wysdom, a separately rubricated ten-line Ten Commandments section, and twelve appended moral lines. The record does not treat the fluid proverb tradition as one stable authorial poem, does not assign the German editorial introduction or notes to the medieval work, and keeps the manuscript, scholarly edition, U-M encoding, and repository XML as separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: The Prouerbis of Wysdom: Rawlinson main component
notes: Zupitza regards lines 129–136 as certainly added and probably connects lines 137–146 with the same later hand or compiler; that analysis is not promoted to separately dated first-class parts here.
DIMEV's abstract record says 106 lines even though its Rawlinson witness description and Zupitza edition contain this 146-line component; the version-level discrepancy remains open.
contentParts.summary: The 146-line rubricated sequence of proverbial and moral counsel, closing with a prayer to the Trinity for mercy.

contentParts.label: The Ten Commandments
notes: DIMEV record 323 / IMEV 176 indexes this five-couplet text independently and records Rawlinson poet. 32 as its sole witness.
contentParts.summary: Ten lines paraphrase the commandments, one commandment per line, under a separate manuscript rubric.

contentParts.label: Appended moral teachings
notes: The MWM record's item 9 explicit runs through line 168, so these appended lines are physically cataloged with the Ten Commandments item even though Zupitza analyzes them as three additional teachings.
contentParts.summary: Twelve further lines urge keeping the commandments, prudent preparation for need, caution after betrayal in lending, and judgment of character under prosperity.

assertion.notes: The witness supplies a terminus ante quem for the exact compilation; its individual couplets may be older.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century (1450–1500), no later than the surviving copy

assertion.notes: This dates the surviving manuscript witness, not the origin of every constituent proverb.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century (1450–1500)

assertion.notes: Zupitza says the verses are printed for the first time as far as he knows; this is not a claim about first publication of every independently circulating couplet.

dateStatements.display: 1893 (first known publication of this Rawlinson compilation)

extent.description: The cataloged medieval text has 168 XML L elements in three LG elements: lines 1–146 Prouerbis, lines 147–156 Ten Commandments, and lines 157–168 appended teachings. The surrounding encoded article additionally has a German preface and 95 prose paragraphs, including 90 note paragraphs; those are representation metrics, not medieval-work extent.

formStatements.description: All 168 cataloged lines are verse, predominantly rhyming couplets; the XML represents them in three LG elements.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The German introduction and notes and the Latin closing rubric are editorial or paratextual languages, not the primary language of the cataloged work.
The preferred title's witness qualifier is supplied for discovery and disambiguation; it is not a manuscript phrase.
The first 146 lines, Ten Commandments, and appended teachings are all Middle English verse; German prose in CME00074 belongs to Zupitza's edition, not the work's form or language.
The manuscript witness date, scribal dialect, and holding location are not silently transferred to every constituent proverb's composition.
The exact physical target is fols. 54v–55r inside the complete manuscript. Any manuscript facsimile should cover the whole physical object and then map this range separately.

next_steps: Use the witness's 1450–1500 date only as a first-attestation and compilation terminus, not as proof that all component lines originated then.
Consult current critical and linguistic scholarship on IMEV 3502, IMEV 176, and the separately indexed couplets.
Do not convert Zupitza's relative-strata argument into absolute dates without new evidence.
openQuestions.question: When were the principal Prouerbis sequence, its later accretions, the Ten Commandments, and the final appended teachings composed or compiled?

next_steps: Keep the proposed authorial dialect region separate from the witness's western Leicestershire or Warwickshire scribal language and from the unknown place of compilation.
Consult current dialectology and inspect a complete target-folio facsimile before raising confidence.
openQuestions.question: Does modern linguistic evidence support Zupitza's tentative Eastern Midlands localization of the principal poet, and where was the exact Rawlinson compilation assembled?

next_steps: Preserve this manuscript-specific compilation as the current unit.
Map independently indexed couplets only when their boundaries and identities materially aid discovery.
Do not list every couplet separately merely because it has parallels elsewhere.
openQuestions.question: Should future metadata model more of the independently circulating couplets as child works, and why does DIMEV's 106-line Prouerbis description differ from this witness's 146-line main component?

next_steps: Retain DIMEV v1.0's inclusion and Edden and Thompson's exclusion as conflicting versioned claims.
Compare the Longleat text against the four substantially related versions before changing the witness census.
openQuestions.question: Is the 64-line Longleat MS 29 text a variant witness of IMEV 3502 or an unrelated proverb sequence?

assertion.notes: Zupitza infers the probable poet's regional affiliation from rhyme evidence; this is not evidence for the physical place of composition, compilation, copying, or codex production.

notes: The MWM witness record instead reports western Leicestershire or Warwickshire scribal language and gives manuscript origin as unknown; the two claims describe different layers.

assertion.notes: Anonymous is retained as the display authorship, but the compilation likely combines circulating materials rather than preserving one stable authorial poem.
Edden and Thompson's fluid-text model and the manuscript's separately rubricated appended material make compiler the most useful whole-unit responsibility.

notes: This responsibility describes the immediate scholarly edition represented by CME00074, not medieval intellectual authorship.

scope.description: German introduction and comparative notes encoded with the medieval text

summaries.value: A manuscript-specific sequence of rhymed counsel opens with a wise man's advice to his sons on fearing God, virtue, reputation, friendship, patience, speech, wealth, humility, charity, and prudent conduct. Later lines turn toward Christian devotion and prayer. A separate rubric introduces a compact verse paraphrase of the Ten Commandments, followed by twelve more teachings on sin, need, lending, false friendship, and the revelation of character through prosperity.

Part 1 contains 146 lines under the Prouerbis of Wysdom rubric. Part 2 contains ten lines, one for each commandment, under its own rubric. Part 3 contains twelve appended moral lines. Zupitza argues for still finer accretional layers within lines 129–168, but the present record keeps the three directly legible textual groupings while recording his hypothesis.

The record treats the Rawlinson sequence as a compilation because related manuscripts contain substantially different arrangements and materials, individual couplets circulate elsewhere, the Ten Commandments has a separate rubric and index identity, and modern scholarship cautions against assuming a single stable authorial original for verse-proverb colonies.

assertion.notes: The witness qualifier is supplied to distinguish this exact 168-line compilation from other materially different Prouerbis versions.

titles.value: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)

titles.value: This folowyng ben the Prouerbis of Wysdom

scope.description: Name in the manuscript closing rubric

titles.value: The wyse man sayd to his sonnes: Thynke on the prouerbis, þat after comys
God, that syttyþe in trynyte, On vs al haue mercy and pyte
This folowyng ben þe x. comawndmentis
All false goddis þou shalt forsak, His name nott yn idell take
When man hath, what his wyll is, Then shewyth he, what he is

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Compilation and first known attestation: second half of the fifteenth century (1450–1500); exact composition dates unknown
work.displayTitle: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)
work.preferredTitle: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)
work.summary: A manuscript-specific sequence of rhymed counsel opens with a wise man's advice to his sons on fearing God, virtue, reputation, friendship, patience, speech, wealth, humility, charity, and prudent conduct. Later lines turn toward Christian devotion and prayer. A separate rubric introduces a compact verse paraphrase of the Ten Commandments, followed by twelve more teachings on sin, need, lending, false friendship, and the revelation of character through prosperity.
